old_claim.note: Advanced Chemistry provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Understanding and controlling chromium content, carbon level, alloy chemistry, and passive-film corrosion behavior enables stainless steel, but chemistry is modeled as an enabling knowledge base rather than a single hard component.

source_shape.source_locator: npj Materials Degradation article introduction states that stainless steel corrosion resistance is owed to a passive film formed on the surface and describes the chromium-rich oxide/oxyhydroxide passive film.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The corrosion-resistance mechanism of stainless steel depends on chromium-rich passive-film chemistry and alloy composition.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports chemistry as an enabling knowledge base for stainless steel while avoiding a required edge because the hard material prerequisite is steel production.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved edge type: advanced_chemistry remains enabling but is now source-checked.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to Brearley's accidental discovery only and excludes modern passive-film understanding.
A more specific alloy-chemistry/passivation node replaces advanced_chemistry as the direct prerequisite.
short_reason: Chromium/passive-film chemistry is the source-backed enabling mechanism for stainless steel corrosion resistance.
